From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Annual association football tournament in Italy

This article is about the men's Italian association football tournament. For other uses, see [Coppa Italia (disambiguation)](/wiki/Coppa_Italia_(disambiguation) "Coppa Italia (disambiguation)").

Football tournament

Coppa Italia

**Coppa Italia** (lit. 'Italy Cup') is the annual [domestic cup](/wiki/Domestic_cup "Domestic cup") of [Italian football](/wiki/Italian_football "Italian football"). The [knockout competition](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament") was organized by the [DDS](/wiki/Direttorio_Divisioni_Superiori "Direttorio Divisioni Superiori") and the [Lega Calcio](/wiki/Lega_Nazionale_Professionisti "Lega Nazionale Professionisti") until [the 2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Coppa_Italia "2009–10 Coppa Italia") and by [Lega Serie A](/wiki/Lega_Serie_A "Lega Serie A") ever since.[[1]](#cite_note-drog-1)

[Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") is the competition's most successful club with fifteen wins, followed by [Roma](/wiki/A.S._Roma "A.S. Roma") and [Inter Milan](/wiki/Inter_Milan "Inter Milan") with nine. Juventus has contested the most finals with 22, followed by Roma with 17 finals. The holder can wear a [cockade of Italy](/wiki/Cockade_of_Italy "Cockade of Italy") (Italian: *coccarda*), akin to the [roundels](/wiki/Roundel "Roundel") that appear on military aircraft. The winner automatically qualifies for both the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") league phase and the [Supercoppa Italiana](/wiki/Supercoppa_Italiana "Supercoppa Italiana") the following year.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coppa_Italia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The beginning of the tournament was turbulent, due to the complexity of the participation of the teams in the tournament, since its inception in 1921, the Italian championship was divided into two groups. On the one hand the rich CCI Championship (Italian Football Confederation) and on the other the poor [FIGC championship](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") ([Italian Football Federation](/wiki/Italian_Football_Federation "Italian Football Federation")). Losing all its most prestigious clubs, the FIGC tried to enhance its rump season with a new cup. The tournament's first edition held in 1922 was won by [F.C. Vado](/wiki/F.C._Vado "F.C. Vado").[[2]](#cite_note-drogster-2) The following agreement between the contenders did not contemplate a cup that, outside a [failed 1926–27 tournament](/wiki/1926%E2%80%9327_Coppa_Italia "1926–27 Coppa Italia") which was cancelled during the round of 32, was not held until [1935–36](/wiki/1935%E2%80%9336_Coppa_Italia "1935–36 Coppa Italia"). The events of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") interrupted the tournament after the 1942–43 season, and it did not resume again until 1958. Since then, it has been played annually or seasonally.[[2]](#cite_note-drogster-2)

The eight seasons during the [fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") period were contested copying the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") format. There was a different trophy, and the winners were awarded the tricolour [scudetto](/wiki/Scudetto "Scudetto") while the championship winners obtained a [Savoyard](/wiki/House_of_Savoy "House of Savoy") scudetto instead.

The present-day cup and cockade were introduced in 1958. The cup was resumed following the voices of the creation of a [Cup Winners Cup](/wiki/Cup_Winners_Cup "Cup Winners Cup"). Having the sole goal to define a participant to the new UEFA competition, the cup had a minimal direct elimination format.

In 1967, following the reduction of the Serie A to 16 teams, semifinals and finals were replaced by a final post-season group, while the following year a pre-season group substituted the early rounds. In 1971, the format was restructured with two semifinal post-season groups, in order to introduce a fixed one-legged final in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").

Ordinary quarterfinals and semi-finals were reintroduced in 1978, with a round of 16 when the competition was reopened to some Serie C clubs. Direct elimination then replaced any group when the Serie A was expanded to 18 club in the late eighties. After the expansion of the league to 20 clubs in the 2000s, the actual minimalist format was fixed.

## Format

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coppa_Italia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Format")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Coccarda_Coppa_Italia.svg/250px-Coccarda_Coppa_Italia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Coccarda_Coppa_Italia.svg)

The *[Coccarda](/wiki/Cockade_of_Italy#Sport "Cockade of Italy")*, the winner's patch

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Gianluigi_Buffon_%2831784615942%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Gianluigi_Buffon_%2831784615942%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gianluigi_Buffon_(31784615942)_(cropped).jpg)

[Gianluigi Buffon](/wiki/Gianluigi_Buffon "Gianluigi Buffon") in 2016, wearing the *Coccarda* won with [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") the season before. Also present is the *[Scudetto](/wiki/Scudetto "Scudetto")*, worn by the holders of the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") title.

The competition is a knockout tournament with pairings for each round made in advance; the draw for the whole competition is made before a ball is kicked. Each tie is played as a single leg, except a [two-legged](/wiki/Two-legged_tie "Two-legged tie") semi-final stage. Through the [2023–24 edition](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Coppa_Italia "2023–24 Coppa Italia"), extra time was played following all matches drawn after 90 minutes. Beginning with [the 2024–25 edition](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Coppa_Italia "2024–25 Coppa Italia"), extra time is only possible in the semi-finals and the final; matches drawn after 90 minutes in all other rounds are immediately followed by a [penalty shoot-out](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)") to determine which team will advance to the next round.

In addition to being presented with the trophy, the winning team also qualifies for the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"). If the winners have already qualified for the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") via the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") or are not entitled to play in UEFA competitions for any reason, the place goes to the next highest placed team in the league table.

There are a total of seven rounds in the competition. It begins in August with the preliminary round and is contested only by the eight lowest-ranked clubs. Clubs playing in [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") join in during the first round with the 12 lowest-ranked teams in [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") based on the previous league season's positions (unless they are to compete in European competition that year) begin the competition in the first round before August is over. The remaining eight Serie A teams join the competition in the third round in winter, at which point 16 teams remain. The quarterfinals are then played in quick succession, and the semi-finals are played in April, before the final in May. The two-legged final was eliminated for [the 2007–08 edition](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Coppa_Italia "2007–08 Coppa Italia") and a single-match final is now played at the [Stadio Olimpico](/wiki/Stadio_Olimpico "Stadio Olimpico") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coppa_Italia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

This is a list of [television broadcasters](/wiki/Television_station "Television station") and [streaming television](/wiki/Streaming_television "Streaming television") providers which provide coverage of the Coppa Italia,[[6]](#cite_note-6) as well as the [Supercoppa Italiana](/wiki/Supercoppa_Italiana "Supercoppa Italiana") and maybe exclude the [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") matches (depending on broadcasting rights in selected regions).

### 2024–2027

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coppa_Italia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2024–2027")]

#### Italy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coppa_Italia&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Italy")]

The Coppa Italia and the Supercoppa Italiana has been broadcast by [Mediaset](/wiki/Mediaset "Mediaset") since the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_in_Italian_football "2021–22 in Italian football"). Previously, the tournament was aired by the national public broadcaster [RAI](/wiki/RAI "RAI") up until the [2020–21 edition](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Coppa_Italia "2020–21 Coppa Italia").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Coppa_Italia&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: International")]
